questions.question: Describe the role and key responsibilities of the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) in a Swing application. Why is it crucial to perform all UI updates on this thread?
keyPoints: The EDT is a single, dedicated thread that handles all events from the native GUI toolkit and processes them.
All event handling and code that updates the GUI must execute on the EDT to ensure thread safety.
Violating this rule by updating the UI from another thread can lead to unpredictable behavior, graphical glitches, and application crashes.
Long-running tasks should be executed on a separate worker thread (e.g., using SwingWorker) to prevent the UI from freezing.

questions.question: Explain the fundamental purpose of a Layout Manager in Swing and name two common ones, briefly describing the type of layout they provide.
keyPoints: A Layout Manager automates the positioning and sizing of components within a container, making the GUI adaptable to different screen sizes and resolutions.
BorderLayout divides a container into five regions: North, South, East, West, and Center. It is the default layout for JFrame and JDialog.
FlowLayout arranges components in a left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow, similar to a paragraph of text. It is the default layout for JPanel.
Using layout managers is preferred over absolute positioning (setBounds) for creating flexible and platform-independent user interfaces.

questions.question: Detail the complete process, from class declaration to display, for creating a simple Swing application with a JFrame that contains a JButton. Your answer should include the key steps for ensuring proper thread safety and application termination.
keyPoints: The UI should be constructed and displayed on the Event Dispatch Thread by wrapping the creation code in a Runnable passed to EventQueue.invokeLater().
The main class should extend JFrame (or create an instance of it) and set properties such as title, default close operation (JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE), and size.
Components (like a JButton) are created and added to the JFrame's content pane.
An ActionListener is registered with the JButton to handle click events.
The JFrame is made visible at the end of the setup process using setVisible(true).
Explanation of why EXIT_ON_CLOSE is important for terminating the application completely when the window is closed.
